---
title: "Day 2: Command Line Interface (CLI) and Your AI Tutor"
subtitle: "Morning brief, Tuesday July 14"
author: "Dan Negrut"
date: 2026-07-14
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../theme.scss]
    slide-number: true
    width: 1250
    margin: 0.08
    transition: fade
    footer: "ProCSI C Training  |  Day 2"
    embed-resources: true
---

## Where this fits

- Almost everything this camp does runs through the **terminal**: git, Python, conda, PyChrono.
    - Terminal: also known as command line interface (CLI)
    - All these commands -- conda, PyChrono, etc. -- are executed from the terminal.

\

- Today you also get comfortable driving the computer by typing, and you meet the helper you'll lean on all camp: **Claude**.
- No prior experience needed. Poke at things and see what happens.

## Today's target

- **By 4:00, your team can:** open a terminal, move around the filesystem, create / rename / delete files and folders, and `git clone` a repository, all from the command line. 
- **Bonus:** you've had a real back-and-forth with Claude and used it to figure something out on your own.
- Herein, I say "Claude", but "ChatGPT" is fine, too.

## How to start

- Open a terminal: **Terminal** on macOS, **Git Bash** or **Windows Terminal** on Windows.
- **No starter today.** Start from a blank prompt; exploring is the point.
- Your one concrete fetch, and how you get all camp materials:
  `git clone https://github.com/DanNegrut/procsi-c-training.git`
- Meet Claude, and try asks like:
  - "I'm on [macOS / Windows]. Explain `pwd`, `ls`, `cd`, `mkdir` with tiny examples."
  - Paste any error you hit and ask "what does this mean and how do I fix it?"

## Command map {.smaller}

- `pwd` where am I &nbsp;•&nbsp; `ls` what's here &nbsp;•&nbsp; `cd <dir>` move in &nbsp;•&nbsp; `cd ..` up one
- `mkdir <name>` make a folder &nbsp;•&nbsp; `touch <file>` make a file
- `mv <a> <b>` rename or move &nbsp;•&nbsp; `rm <file>` delete (careful, no undo)
- `git clone <url>` copy a repository onto your machine
- Handy: **Tab** completes names, **Up arrow** recalls your last command

## What is an IDE? {.smaller}

- An **IDE** (Integrated Development Environment) is one app that bundles everything for writing code: a smart text editor, a file browser, and a **built-in terminal**.
- You could write code in Notepad; an IDE is the workshop version, with every tool within arm's reach.
- Ours is **VS Code**: free, runs on every OS, and the most widely used code editor in the world. (Others exist, like PyCharm and Cursor; same idea.)
- Starting tomorrow, you write your Python in VS Code and run it from its built-in terminal, using exactly today's command-line skills.

## Install VS Code {.smaller}

- Install it **today**, on every laptop your team will write code on:
  - **Windows and macOS**: installer from [code.visualstudio.com](https://code.visualstudio.com); the defaults are fine.
  - **Linux**: `sudo snap install code --classic` (or grab the .deb/.rpm from the same site).
- First steps once it opens:
  - **File, Open Folder**, and pick the `procsi-c-training` folder you cloned this morning.
  - Open the built-in terminal: **Ctrl + the backtick key** (top-left of the keyboard, under Esc). Everything from today's command map works there.
- Tomorrow VS Code will suggest the **Python extension**; say yes.

## Your end-of-day talk

- ~10 minutes: what you explored, the handiest command you found, and one thing Claude helped you understand.
- **Start or Close with your team's quote of the day.**
- Stretch goals:
    - Find and explain one command not on the map above (for example `cat`, `grep`, or `history`).
    - If you or a teammate have a **paid** Claude plan (Pro or higher): install the **Claude Code** extension in VS Code (Extensions panel, search "Claude Code", publisher Anthropic), and have it generate a script that counts the characters in a file. Heads up: the free claude.ai plan does not include Claude Code, so the browser tutor stays your main tool.
